We comply with all CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board) guidelines for safe e-waste handling, data destruction, and disposal. Corporate clients can verify our authorization and request compliance documentation at any time.

whyTerionix.heading: Built for Enterprise
whyTerionix.subtitle: Three pillars that make Terionix the trusted partner for corporate e-waste management.

pillars.title: Data Security
pillars.body: We don't just recycle your hardware; we eliminate your data liability. We provide a tamper-proof, video-graphed data destruction process with serialized Blancco certificates that stand up to any global security audit.

pillars.title: Legal Compliance
pillars.body: Under the E-Waste Management Rules, your company is legally liable if your waste ends up in a landfill. We handle Form 6 manifests, provide CPCB-authorized recycling certificates, and insulate your brand from regulatory penalties.

pillars.title: Green PR / ESG
pillars.body: We provide a quarterly 'Green Dashboard' for your annual BRSR report. We don't just give you a bill; we tell you exactly how many tons of carbon emissions your company averted by recycling with Terionix.

solutions.intro: End-to-end e-waste management tailored for businesses, institutions, and government agencies. From compliant collection to certified destruction, we make responsible recycling seamless.

sections.title: E-Waste Collection & Logistics
sections.body: Our reverse logistics network covers the entire Tamil Nadu region. We offer scheduled pickups, one-time bulk collections, and permanent drop-off points. Every shipment is GPS-tracked, carefully packed, and transported in compliance with hazardous material handling guidelines.
features: Scheduled and on-demand pickup services
GPS-tracked transport with chain of custody
Secure packing for sensitive equipment
Bulk collection drives for corporates and institutions
PAN-Tamil Nadu coverage with expanding pan-India network

sections.title: Data Destruction & Security
sections.body: Before any device is recycled, all data is destroyed using certified methods. We offer on-site degaussing, physical shredding, and software-based erasure — each with a verifiable audit trail and certificate of destruction.
features: NSA/CSS-compliant degaussing for magnetic media
Physical shredding (hard drives, SSDs, tapes)
Software-based data erasure (DoD 5220.22-M standard)
On-site destruction available for sensitive clients
Certificate of destruction with serial number tracking

sections.title: EPR Compliance Support
sections.body: We help producers meet their Extended Producer Responsibility obligations under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022. Our end-to-end compliance package covers documentation, collection targets, recycling certificates, and annual reporting.
features: Complete EPR documentation and filing support
Collection target fulfillment with verified channelization
Quarterly and annual compliance reporting
Dedu credit management and transfer
Audit-ready records for CPCB/SPCB inspections

sections.title: Material Recovery
sections.body: Our processing facility recovers valuable materials from e-waste using a combination of mechanical sorting, density separation, and hydrometallurgical processes. Recovered materials are sent directly to refineries and manufacturers, closing the loop in the circular economy.
features: Multi-stage mechanical sorting and shredding
Precious metal recovery (gold, silver, palladium)
Base metal recovery (copper, aluminum, tin)
Plastic recycling and compounding
Hazardous component safe disposal

sections.title: Compliance & Certification
sections.body: We provide full compliance documentation for your annual environmental audits, including CPCB-compliant Green Certificates of Recycling and Form 6 manifests (Hazardous & E-Waste Manifest) as required under the E-Waste (Management) Rules issued by the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC) and Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB). Corporate clients can legally write off assets only with proper Form 6 documentation and a Certificate of Disposal/Recycling from an authorized recycler — we provide both.
features: CPCB-compliant Green Certificates of Recycling for every batch processed
Form 6 Manifest (Hazardous & E-Waste) for corporate asset write-off
Certificate of Data Destruction with serial number tracking
Certificate of Disposal/Recycling for regulatory audit trails
Full EPR documentation support under E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022
Quarterly compliance reports for CPCB/SPCB inspections
Dedu credit management and transfer documentation

sections.title: R&D Labs

highlights: Partnerships with academic institutions for recycling research
Patent-pending processes for rare earth recovery

sections.title: Future Technologies
sections.body: We're exploring next-generation recycling technologies including AI-powered sorting systems, robotic disassembly lines, and bioleaching — using microorganisms to extract metals from PCBs. These technologies will define the future of e-waste recycling.
highlights: AI-based waste stream classification and sorting
Bioleaching research for precious metal extraction

sections.title: Circular Economy Models
sections.body: Beyond recycling, we champion circular economy principles — designing out waste, keeping materials in use, and regenerating natural systems. We work with manufacturers to design for recyclability and help businesses transition to circular procurement models.
highlights: Circular procurement advisory for corporate clients
Refurbishment and resale programs for functional equipment

impactInsights.intro: Transparency is at the core of what we do. Explore our environmental impact metrics, learn about e-waste, and access resources to make informed decisions.

sections.title: Sustainability Impact
sections.body: Every device we recycle contributes to measurable environmental good. Our impact tracking system monitors every metric — from kilograms of e-waste processed to tonnes of CO₂ emissions prevented. We publish quarterly impact reports for full transparency.

sections.title: E-Waste Facts
sections.body: E-waste is the world's fastest-growing waste stream. Understanding the scale of the problem — and the opportunity — is the first step toward meaningful action.

E-waste accounts for 70% of the toxic waste in landfills, yet represents only 2% of landfill volume.

segments.text: is a forward-thinking e-waste management company committed to solving the growing problem of electronic waste. We provide comprehensive solutions for businesses, governments, and individuals who want to dispose of their electronic waste responsibly.

Founded with a vision to create a cleaner, greener future, Terionix operates across the entire e-waste lifecycle — from collection and transportation to sorting, processing, and certified disposal. We ensure that every piece of electronic waste is tracked, handled, and recycled in full compliance with environmental regulations.

is to reduce the environmental and health hazards caused by improper e-waste disposal. We believe in the principles of the circular economy — recovering valuable materials from end-of-life electronics and reintroducing them into the supply chain.

Whether you are a corporation clearing out old IT equipment, a manufacturer managing production waste, or an individual dropping off a pile of old chargers, Terionix has a solution tailored to your needs.

certifications.title: TNPCB Authorized
certifications.description: Registered e-waste recycler under Tamil Nadu Pollution Control Board

certifications.title: CPCB Compliant
certifications.description: Full compliance with Central Pollution Control Board guidelines

segments.text: began its journey with a simple belief — that electronic waste should not end up in landfills ever. What started as a small initiative by a group of environmental enthusiasts has grown into a full-fledged e-waste management company serving hundreds of clients across the region.

In our early years, we focused on awareness — reaching out to schools, offices, and communities to educate people about the dangers of improper e-waste disposal. We organized collection drives and partnered with local municipalities to set up drop-off points.

, we invested in building a proper processing facility equipped with shredding, sorting, and material recovery equipment. Today, we have the infrastructure to handle large volumes of e-waste safely and efficiently.

Looking ahead, Terionix aims to expand its reach, develop partnerships with more manufacturers, and continue pushing for better e-waste infrastructure across the country. Our goal is to make responsible e-waste disposal the norm, not the exception.

learn.courseSubtitle: A beginner-friendly course on understanding e-waste and taking action for a cleaner planet.

moduleList.title: What is E-Waste?
moduleList.desc: Learn what counts as e-waste and understand the scale of the global problem.

moduleList.title: The Hidden Value
moduleList.desc: Discover the precious materials hidden inside your old devices and why mining, not dumping, is the future.

moduleList.title: Environmental Impact
moduleList.desc: Understand how improper e-waste disposal affects our planet, wildlife, and human health.

dashboard.heading: Welcome to E-Waste Recycling 101
dashboard.body: This course will take you from the basics of what e-waste is, through the hidden value in your old devices, the environmental impact of improper disposal, and how recycling works — all the way to taking real action. Each module builds on the last, and you can track your progress as you go.

whatIsEwaste.heroSubtitle: Before we can solve a problem, we need to understand it. Let's start with the basics — what exactly counts as e-waste, how much there is, and why it's different from regular trash.

sections.title: Defining E-Waste
sections.body: E-waste (electronic waste) refers to any discarded electrical or electronic device — anything with a plug, battery, or circuit board that's reached the end of its useful life. This includes everything from a tiny smartwatch to a full-sized refrigerator. When these devices are thrown in the trash instead of being properly recycled, they release toxic substances that harm our environment and health.

items.label: Phones & Tablets
items.desc: Smartphones, tablets, e-readers, wearables

items.label: Computers & Laptops
items.desc: Desktops, laptops, servers, hard drives

items.label: Displays
items.desc: TVs, monitors, LED screens, projectors

items.label: Batteries & Power
items.desc: Lithium-ion, power banks, UPS, adapters

items.label: Cables & Accessories
items.desc: Chargers, cables, headphones, keyboards

items.label: Large Appliances
items.desc: Refrigerators, ACs, washing machines

items.label: Peripherals
items.desc: Printers, scanners, routers, modems

items.label: Gaming & Entertainment
items.desc: Consoles, controllers, VR headsets

stats.label: e-waste generated in 2024 — that's 2x the weight of all cars sold in the US
e-waste is growing 3 times faster than any other waste stream
of recoverable materials are dumped or burned each year
of the world's e-waste has no formal recycling tracking

sections.title: Why Is E-Waste Growing So Fast?
sections.body: Three main drivers: First, planned obsolescence — devices are designed to be replaced every 2-3 years. Second, falling prices make it cheaper to buy new than repair old. Third, the rise of electronics in every part of our lives — from smart homes to electric vehicles — means more devices exist than ever before. The average person now owns over 10 connected devices, and that number keeps climbing.

sections.title: E-Waste is Different
sections.body: Unlike paper or glass waste, e-waste contains both highly valuable materials (gold, silver, copper) AND highly toxic substances (lead, mercury, cadmium). This dual nature makes proper recycling both economically important and environmentally critical — you can't just throw electronics in a landfill.

hiddenValue.heroTitle: The Hidden Value in Your Drawer
hiddenValue.heroSubtitle: That drawer full of old phones and cables isn't just clutter — it's an urban gold mine. Let's look at what's actually inside our electronic devices.

sections.title: Your Devices Are Treasure Chests
sections.body: Every electronic device contains a surprising amount of valuable materials. A typical smartphone contains over 60 different elements from the periodic table — including precious metals like gold, silver, platinum, and palladium, as well as base metals like copper, tin, and aluminum. Even the small amounts add up dramatically when you consider the millions of devices discarded each year.

items.label: Gold
items.desc: Used in circuit boards and connectors for its conductivity. One ton of smartphones = 100x more gold than one ton of ore.

items.label: Silver
items.desc: Found in circuit boards, keyboards, and displays. Essential for electrical contacts.

items.label: Copper
items.desc: The most abundant valuable metal in electronics. Used in wiring, cables, and circuit boards.

items.label: Rare Earth Elements
items.desc: Neodymium, dysprosium, and others used in speakers, vibration motors, and screens.

items.label: Lithium & Cobalt
items.desc: Critical for batteries. Mining them is environmentally destructive — recycling reduces the need.

items.label: Plastics & Glass
items.desc: Up to 40% of a device's weight. Can be recycled into new products, furniture, and building materials.

stats.label: One ton of smartphones has 100x more gold than one ton of gold ore
of materials in a mobile phone can be recovered and reused
Recycling aluminum uses 80% less energy than mining new bauxite
in recycled PCBs than in natural copper ore

sections.title: What is Urban Mining?
sections.body: Urban mining is the concept of 'mining' valuable materials from discarded electronics instead of digging them out of the earth. It's called 'urban' because the resources are in our cities — in our homes, offices, and landfills. The concentration of precious metals in e-waste is often 10 to 100 times higher than in natural ore deposits. Urban mining creates local jobs, reduces environmental destruction from mining, and keeps valuable materials in the economy.

sections.title: The Bottom Line
sections.body: Your old phone is not trash — it's a resource. The $91 billion worth of recoverable materials thrown away each year represents both an environmental tragedy and an enormous economic opportunity.

environmentalImpact.heroSubtitle: What happens when e-waste ends up in landfills? The consequences are far more serious than most people realize.

sections.title: The Hidden Cost of 'Out of Sight'
sections.body: When electronics are thrown in the trash, they end up in landfills where they're crushed under tons of garbage. Over time, rain water filters through the landfill, creating a toxic liquid called leachate that carries dangerous chemicals into the soil and groundwater. Meanwhile, burning e-waste releases toxic fumes into the air. The problem is global — an estimated 80% of e-waste is either landfilled or informally recycled in developing countries under unsafe conditions.

items.label: Lead
items.desc: Found in solder and CRT monitors. Damages the nervous system and kidneys.

items.label: Mercury
items.desc: Used in flat-screen displays and switches. Highly toxic, accumulates in the food chain.

items.label: Cadmium
items.desc: Found in batteries and semiconductors. Carcinogenic, accumulates in organs.

items.label: Brominated Flame Retardants
items.desc: Added to plastic casings. Disrupts hormones and persists in the environment.

items.label: Beryllium
items.desc: Used in circuit boards. Causes chronic lung disease when inhaled.

items.label: PVC Plastics
items.desc: Cable insulation. Releases dioxins when burned — among the most toxic known substances.

items.label: Water Contamination
items.desc: Toxic leachate seeps into groundwater, affecting drinking water supplies for communities near landfills.

items.label: Soil Degradation
items.desc: Heavy metals accumulate in soil, making it infertile and entering the food chain through crops.

items.label: Air Pollution
items.desc: Burning e-waste releases dioxins, furans, and heavy metals into the air we breathe.

items.label: Human Health
items.desc: Communities near e-waste sites face higher rates of cancer, respiratory disease, and birth defects.

sections.title: E-Waste and Climate Change
sections.body: The connection between e-waste and climate change is often overlooked. Mining and processing virgin materials for electronics is energy-intensive, contributing significant greenhouse gas emissions. When e-waste decomposes in landfills, it releases methane — a greenhouse gas 25 times more potent than CO₂. And manufacturing new electronics from scratch generates far more emissions than using recycled materials. For example, recycling one million laptops saves the energy equivalent of powering 3,500 US homes for an entire year.

sections.title: The Good News
sections.body: Every device you recycle makes a measurable difference. Proper recycling prevents toxic materials from entering the environment, saves energy, reduces mining, and cuts greenhouse gas emissions. Your actions matter — and they add up.

theProcess.heroSubtitle: What actually happens to your device after you drop it off for recycling? Follow the journey from your home to becoming new products.

sections.title: A Journey of Transformation
sections.body: E-waste recycling is a sophisticated, multi-stage process that combines human expertise with cutting-edge technology. Certified recyclers like Terionix follow strict protocols to ensure every component is handled safely and every recoverable material is captured. Here's how it works.

steps.title: Collection & Transport
steps.body: E-waste is collected from homes, offices, schools, and drop-off centers. Certified transporters safely move it to the recycling facility with full tracking documentation.

steps.title: Manual Sorting
steps.body: Items are sorted by type. Working devices are set aside for refurbishment and resale — extending their life. Non-working items are sorted into categories: large appliances, small electronics, cables, batteries, etc.

steps.title: Dismantling
steps.body: Skilled technicians take devices apart by hand. Batteries are removed (they require special handling). Circuit boards, hard drives, wiring, and plastic casings are separated into different streams.

steps.title: Shredding & Separation
steps.body: Materials are fed into powerful shredders that break them into tiny pieces. Advanced technologies — magnets, eddy currents, optical sensors, and density separators — sort the materials into pure streams: ferrous metals, non-ferrous metals, plastics, and glass.

steps.title: Material Recovery
steps.body: Pure materials are processed further. Metals are smelted into ingots. Plastics are melted into pellets. Precious metals like gold and silver are extracted using chemical and electrolytic processes. These raw materials are sold to manufacturers.

steps.title: New Life
steps.body: Recovered materials become new products. Gold from circuit boards goes into new electronics. Copper from wiring becomes plumbing or cables. Plastic pellets become furniture, auto parts, or even new electronics. The circle is complete.

stats.label: of e-waste materials can be recovered with modern recycling technology
compared to manufacturing new products from virgin materials
of water saved per ton of electronics recycled instead of mined
recycling aluminum requires 80% less energy than mining new ore

sections.title: Certified Recycling Matters
sections.body: Not all recycling is equal. Certified recyclers like Terionix follow strict standards for data destruction, worker safety, and environmental protection. When you choose a certified recycler, you can be confident that your devices won't end up in a landfill or be shipped to developing countries for unsafe processing. Always ask for certification when choosing an e-waste recycler.

quiz.heroSubtitle: You've learned a lot! Now let's see how much you remember. Answer these 8 questions covering everything from the course.

questions.question: What does 'e-waste' refer to?
options: Any discarded electronic device with a plug or battery
questions.explanation: E-waste includes any discarded electrical or electronic device — anything with a plug, battery, or circuit board.

questions.question: How much e-waste was generated globally in 2024?
questions.explanation: A record 62 million metric tons of e-waste was generated in 2024 — that's growing 3x faster than any other waste stream.

questions.question: What percentage of e-waste is formally recycled worldwide?
questions.explanation: Only about 17% of the world's e-waste is formally collected and recycled. The other 78%+ is unaccounted for — landfilled, burned, or informally processed.

questions.question: How much more gold is in one ton of smartphones vs. one ton of gold ore?
questions.explanation: Smartphones have about 100 times more gold per ton than natural gold ore — making urban mining incredibly efficient!

questions.question: Which toxic substance is commonly found in flat-screen displays?
questions.explanation: Mercury is used in flat-screen displays and switches. It's highly toxic and accumulates in the food chain.

questions.question: What is 'urban mining'?
options: Recovering valuable materials from discarded electronics
questions.explanation: Urban mining is the process of recovering valuable materials like gold, silver, and copper from discarded electronics instead of mining them from the earth.

questions.question: About how much of a mobile phone's materials can be recovered?
questions.explanation: Up to 95% of materials in a mobile phone can be recovered and reused, including precious metals, copper, plastics, and glass.

questions.question: Why is certified recycling important?
options: It ensures safe handling, data destruction, and environmental standards
questions.explanation: Certified recyclers follow strict standards for worker safety, environmental protection, and data security — ensuring your devices are handled responsibly.

takeAction.heroSubtitle: Knowledge is only the first step. Now it's time to turn what you've learned into real action. Choose your commitments and start making a difference today.

sections.title: Your Actions Matter
sections.body: E-waste recycling starts with individual choices. Every device you recycle instead of trash, every friend you educate, every brand you question about their sustainability practices — it all adds up. When millions of people make the right choice, the impact is enormous.

items.label: Find a Certified Recycler
items.desc: Search for TNPCB/CPCB authorized recyclers in your area. Look for certifications and read reviews.

items.label: Prepare Your Devices
items.desc: Back up your data, sign out of accounts, factory reset where possible. For hard drives, ask about data destruction.

items.label: Drop Off or Schedule Pickup
items.desc: Many recyclers offer free drop-off or scheduled pickups. Bundle your e-waste to minimize trips.

items.label: Get a Certificate
items.desc: Ask for a certificate of recycling or data destruction for your records — especially for business devices.

items.label: Spread the Word
items.desc: Tell friends, family, and colleagues. Share what you've learned. Collective action creates real change.

pledge.title: Take the Pledge
pledge.subtitle: Make a personal commitment to be part of the solution.
commitments: I will recycle all my old electronics responsibly instead of throwing them in the trash.
I will research and use certified e-waste recyclers in my area.
I will educate at least one friend or family member about e-waste recycling.
I will consider repairability and sustainability when buying new electronics.
I will support brands that design for recyclability and take responsibility for their products.
